 {"id":"asupersync-xrc","title":"[EPIC-PHASE] Phase 1 - Parallel Scheduler and Region Heap","description":"## Overview
Phase 1 extends the single-threaded deterministic kernel (Phase 0) to support parallel execution with work-stealing, region-isolated heaps, and multi-threaded scheduling while preserving all invariants.

## Goals
1. Enable true parallelism for throughput
2. Maintain determinism in lab runtime (parallel simulation)
3. Implement region heap for memory isolation
4. Work-stealing scheduler for load balancing
5. All Phase 0 invariants MUST be preserved

## Key Components

### 1. Work-Stealing Scheduler
- Per-worker local queues
- Global steal queue for overflow
- Lock-free deque implementation
- Maintain scheduling determinism via virtual schedule in lab mode

### 2. Region Heap
- Each region owns its allocations
- Mass deallocation on region close
- Thread-safe allocation within region
- Region-local bump allocator (fast path)
- Fallback to global allocator

### 3. Parallel Task Model
- `Task<T>` is `Send` - can migrate between workers
- Wake deduplication across threads
- Atomic task state transitions
- Thread-safe RegionRecord access

### 4. Synchronization Primitives (Parallel Versions)
- Lock-free MPSC queue
- Sharded counters for obligations
- Atomic region state machine

## Dependencies
- Requires complete Phase 0 kernel
- Requires all Phase 0 invariants proven/tested
- Requires two-phase primitives from Phase 0

## Constraints
- No additional invariants beyond Phase 0
- Must support lab runtime schedule replay
- Must support deterministic parallel simulation
- Cannot break cancel-correctness

## Non-Goals for Phase 1
- I/O integration (Phase 2)
- Actor model (Phase 3)
- Distributed execution (Phase 4)
- DPOR/TLA+ tooling (Phase 5)

## Mathematical Foundation
From the spec:
- Work-stealing preserves eventual quiescence
- Region heap uses arena allocation semantics
- Parallel near-semiring: join/race laws still hold under parallelism

## Testing Strategy
- All Phase 0 tests must pass
- Add parallel stress tests
- Add work-stealing correctness tests
- Verify determinism under parallel lab runtime

## References
- asupersync_plan_v4.md: §7 Phase 1 (Parallel)
- Chase-Lev work-stealing deque
- Region-based memory management (Tofte-Talpin)

## Success Criteria
- Parallel scheduler executes `Send` tasks across multiple workers while preserving Phase 0 invariants.
- Region heap enables safe region-owned allocation and quiescent reclamation on close.
- Lab runtime can deterministically *simulate* multi-worker schedules (repeatable traces).
- Stress/E2E tests cover work stealing, cancellation drain, and quiescence under contention.

 {"id":"asupersync-xrc.1","title":"Phase 1: Work-Stealing Scheduler","description":"# Phase 1: Work-Stealing Scheduler

## Purpose
Extend Phase 0’s single-thread scheduler to a parallel work-stealing scheduler while preserving all invariants and retaining deterministic lab behavior.

## Core Requirements
- Per-worker local queues (fast path)
- Steal protocol for load balancing
- Global injection queue for external wakes/spawns
- Wake dedup across threads
- Cancel lane priority must remain semantically dominant

## Determinism Constraint
Even if production scheduling is nondeterministic, the lab runtime must be able to:
- simulate multi-worker scheduling deterministically
- replay schedules

This implies the scheduler must expose a “virtual schedule” control surface in lab mode.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Parallel execution preserves:
  - region close ⇒ quiescence
  - cancellation protocol drains
  - losers drained
  - no obligation leaks
- No task can be orphaned due to migration.

## Testing
- Parallel stress tests with deterministic seed.
- Schedule replay tests.
- Work-stealing invariants tests (no lost tasks, no duplicate polls, no starvation).

 {"id":"asupersync-xrc.1.1","title":"Implement Chase-Lev work-stealing deque (core primitive)","description":"# Chase–Lev Work-Stealing Deque

## Purpose
Implement the core work-stealing deque used by each worker:
- owner pushes/pops from the bottom (fast path)
- thieves steal from the top

This is the backbone of parallel scheduling.

## Constraints
- Must be correct under data-race-free Rust (use atomics).
- Must avoid `unsafe` in core if at all possible (unsafe is forbidden by project rules).
  - If a fully lock-free implementation requires unsafe, we must design an alternative:
    - use `Mutex`/`RwLock` in Phase 1 initially (correctness first), then optimize later
    - or encapsulate unsafe behind a separately-audited crate (only if allowed)

Given the project’s `unsafe` prohibition, the plan-of-record should start with a correct, deterministic, safe implementation even if it is not maximally performant.

## Plan-of-Record Options
### Option A: Safe deque with locks (Phase 1 baseline)
- Use `Mutex<VecDeque<TaskId>>` per worker.
- Steal = pop_front.
- Push/pop = push_back/pop_back.
- Determinism in lab is straightforward.

### Option B: Lock-free deque (requires careful review)
- Many lock-free implementations require unsafe and careful memory ordering.
- Likely incompatible with `#![forbid(unsafe_code)]` in core.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Correctness properties:
  - no lost tasks
  - no duplicated tasks
  - owner pop and thief steal interleavings behave correctly
- Deterministic tests simulate concurrent accesses.

## Testing
- Model-based tests:
  - simulate multiple threads performing operations
  - verify resulting multiset of tasks matches expected

-{"id":"asupersync-xrc.1.2","title":"Implement multi-worker 3-lane scheduler with stealing","description":"# Multi-Worker 3-Lane Scheduler (Cancel > Timed > Ready)

## Purpose
Upgrade Phase 0’s scheduler into a multi-worker scheduler that:
- preserves cancel lane semantic priority
- supports stealing for load balancing
- integrates timers and external wakes

## Core Design
Each worker maintains (at minimum):
- cancel lane queue
- timed lane queue (or a shared timer structure)
- ready lane queue

Global/shared structures:
- injection queue for cross-thread wakeups and spawns
- timer heap (may be shared, sharded, or per-worker depending on design)

## Cancel Lane Priority (Non-Negotiable)
Even in parallel, we must ensure:
- cancel work is not starved by ready work
- draining completes within budgets under fairness assumptions

## Acceptance Criteria
- No starvation of cancel lane tasks.
- Stealing never violates ownership invariants.
- Wake dedup across workers prevents duplicate scheduling.

## Testing
- Stress tests with many tasks and forced cancellations.
- Deterministic parallel lab tests to exercise stealing.

","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-16T07:16:11.718854337Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-26T18:10:36.200403934Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"asupersync-xrc.1.2","depends_on_id":"asupersync-akx","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T06:20:45Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"asupersync-xrc.1.2","depends_on_id":"asupersync-xrc.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T06:20:45Z","created_by":"import"}],"comments":[{"id":8,"issue_id":"asupersync-xrc.1.2","author":"MaroonDog (claude-code/opus-4.5)","text":"Implemented core multi-worker 3-lane scheduler:

**New files created:**
- src/runtime/scheduler/global_injector.rs - Lane-aware global injection queue
- src/runtime/scheduler/three_lane.rs - Multi-worker 3-lane scheduler with stealing

**Key features implemented:**
1. GlobalInjector with cancel/timed/ready lanes for cross-thread wakeups
2. Per-worker PriorityScheduler (Arc<Mutex>) for local scheduling
3. ThreeLaneWorker::run_loop() with strict priority ordering: cancel > timed > ready
4. Work stealing only from ready lanes (preserves cancel priority semantics)
5. Lane-specific pop methods in PriorityScheduler (pop_cancel_only, pop_timed_only, pop_ready_only)
6. steal_ready_batch() for batch stealing from other workers

**Acceptance criteria status:**
- [x] Cancel lane tasks never starved (always checked first in run loop)
- [x] Stealing never violates ownership invariants (only steals from ready lane)
- [~] Wake dedup - needs integration with TaskWakeState from record/task.rs

**Remaining work:**
- Integrate with RuntimeState for actual task execution
- Hook up TaskWakeState for wake dedup across workers
- Timer integration for checking timed task deadlines
- Waker implementation that routes to correct lane

All 23 scheduler tests pass. All 2165 total tests pass.
","created_at":"2026-01-26T18:08:12Z"},{"id":9,"issue_id":"asupersync-xrc.1.2","author":"MaroonDog (claude-code/opus-4.5)","text":"**Status Update: Core Implementation Complete**

The core multi-worker 3-lane scheduler infrastructure is now implemented and all tests pass.

**What's Done:**
- GlobalInjector for lane-aware cross-thread wakeups
- ThreeLaneScheduler coordinator
- ThreeLaneWorker with strict priority run loop
- Work stealing from ready lanes only
- All 23 scheduler tests pass

**Integration Work Remaining (can be follow-up beads):**
- Hook up TaskWakeState.notify() when scheduling tasks (prevents duplicate enqueue)
- Implement Waker that routes wakes to correct lane based on TaskState (cancel vs ready)
- Integrate ThreeLaneWorker.execute() with RuntimeState to actually poll futures
- Timer wheel integration for timed lane deadline checking

These integration items may overlap with:
- asupersync-xrc.1.3 (wake dedup - already has TaskWakeState implemented)
- asupersync-9d3 (Work-Stealing Scheduler Core)

Recommend: Create follow-up bead for runtime integration or mark current bead as substantially complete.
","created_at":"2026-01-26T18:10:36Z"}]}

 {"id":"asupersync-xrc.1.3","title":"Implement cross-thread wake dedup and atomic task state transitions","description":"# Cross-Thread Wake Dedup + Atomic Task State

## Purpose
Parallel scheduling requires task state and wake signals to be safe under concurrency.

Phase 0 can use plain booleans and single-thread invariants. Phase 1 needs:
- atomic wake flags
- atomic task lifecycle transitions (or lock-protected transitions)
- clear happens-before relationships for trace correctness

## Constraints
- Core crate forbids unsafe code.
- Prefer correctness and determinism over extreme lock-free optimization.

## Plan-of-Record
- Start with lock-protected task records (e.g., per-task `Mutex`) if needed.
- Ensure wake dedup prevents:
  - double-enqueue
  - missed wakeups

## Acceptance Criteria
- Under heavy contention:
  - no missed wakeups
  - no duplicate polls beyond what semantics allow
  - cancellation transitions remain monotone

## Testing
- Parallel stress tests with randomized but deterministic scheduling in lab.

","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"IvoryCompass","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-16T07:16:19.232391007Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-26T17:06:50.340314082Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"asupersync-xrc.1.3","depends_on_id":"asupersync-akx","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T06:20:45Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"asupersync-xrc.1.3","depends_on_id":"asupersync-xrc.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T06:20:45Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"asupersync-xrc.1.4","title":"Implement admission control/backpressure per region (spawn throttling)","description":"# Admission Control / Backpressure (Per Region)

## Purpose
The design calls out admission control and backpressure as core scheduling features:
- throttle spawn/admission per region
- apply backpressure at reserve points (two-phase effects)
- integrate priority into scheduling/budget decisions

Even if Phase 0 is minimal, we must track this as part of the scheduler’s long-term contract.

## Requirements
- Define region-level limits:
  - max live children
  - max outstanding obligations
- Define backpressure signals surfaced to users:
  - reserve waits
  - spawn returns error or waits depending on policy

## Acceptance Criteria
- Admission control does not break invariants.
- Backpressure is deterministic in lab mode.

","status":"open","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-16T07:30:21.462982234Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-16T07:30:21.462982234Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"asupersync-xrc.1.4","depends_on_id":"asupersync-akx","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-01-27T06:20:45Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"asupersync-xrc.1.4","depends_on_id":"asupersync-xrc.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T06:20:45Z","created_by":"import"}]}
 {"id":"asupersync-xrc.2","title":"Phase 1: Region Heap + Send Task Model","description":"# Phase 1: Region Heap + Send Task Model

## Purpose
Make the “task tier” sound:
- allow `Send` tasks to migrate across workers
- preserve region ownership and lifetimes via region-owned allocation

This is the “soundness frontier” encoded into the runtime:
- fibers (Phase 0): borrow-friendly, same-thread
- tasks (Phase 1): `Send`, parallel, region-heap-backed

## Requirements
- Region-owned allocation arena that is reclaimed only after region close/quiescence.
- A `RRef<'r, T>`-style handle (or equivalent) to store data with region lifetime.
- Clear rules for what can be captured in a migrating task.

## Acceptance Criteria
- It is impossible (by construction or by runtime checks) for a migrating task to hold references that outlive the region heap.
- Region close safely reclaims region heap after quiescence.

## Testing
- Parallel tests that allocate in region heap, migrate tasks, and verify data remains valid.
- Leak tests: ensure region heap reclaimed only after close.

","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-16T07:15:37.330400428Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-27T16:22:21.025337219Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"asupersync-xrc.2","depends_on_id":"asupersync-xrc","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-01-27T06:20:45Z","created_by":"import"}]}
